»Komekami! Girls Rice Mascots« entsteht bei Daily Planets, der Manga Contents Promotion Organization und SO-DO Creative unter der Regie von Akinori Kagamiki Suzuki, das Drehbuch schreibt Hideki Sonoda (Digimon Adventure), das Character Design stammt von U., die Soundregie liegt bei Hirohiko Ebihara und die Animation entsteht mit Unterstützung von Logic Box Pictures im 3DCG-Bereich.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Aus dem alten Sprichwort, dass in jedem Reiskorn sieben Gottheiten wohnen, entsteht eine besondere Geschichte: die „Komekami! Girls“, sieben Schwestern und Kinder der Reisgottheit. Als ihre Mutter, die Reiskönigin, plötzlich in den Himmel zurückgerufen wird und von der Erde getrennt bleibt, verlieren die Schwestern ihren wichtigsten Halt. Um herauszufinden, was geschehen ist, und um die letzten Spuren ihrer Mutter zu finden, ziehen sie los. Ihr Ausgangspunkt ist Usa in Oita, doch ihr Weg führt sie durch ganz Kyushu. Mithilfe von Teleportation springen sie von Schrein zu Schrein, vor allem zu den berühmten Hachiman-Schreinen, und treffen überall auf Menschen mit ganz eigenen Sorgen. Während sie Probleme lösen und anderen helfen, lernen sie zugleich immer mehr über Nahrung, Zutaten und die Bedeutung von Essen. Und weil Lernen allein nicht reicht, stellen sie sich auch Kochduellen, die sie an ihre Grenzen bringen. So wird aus einer Suche eine Reise des Erwachsenwerdens, bei der die sieben Schwestern nicht nur Antworten finden, sondern auch ihre eigene Stärke.

images.alt:
📜 Beschreibung 》
Im 13. Jahrhundert wird das Leben der jungen Sitara durch den Verlust ihrer Mutter und die Trennung von ihrer Heimat zerstört. Ohne Schutz und ohne Zukunft landet sie in den Sklavenmärkten Irans und ist völlig auf sich gestellt. Eine Familie von Gelehrten nimmt sie schließlich auf und in der Stille dieses neuen Alltags lernt Sitara die Macht des Wissens kennen. Währenddessen breitet sich das Mongolische Reich unter Kaiser Dschingis Khan unaufhaltsam aus und verschlingt ein Land nach dem anderen. Als diese grenzenlose Expansion schließlich Sitara’s Stadt erreicht, zerbricht ihr friedlicher Alltag. Von da an beginnen sich die Räder des Schicksals zu drehen, und aus dem verlorenen Mädchen wird eine Figur, die einen ganzen Kontinent beeinflussen kann.

Deense verkiezingen monden uit in nek-aan-nekrace tussen links en rechts

De Deense parlementsverkiezingen, die premier Mette Frederiksen vervroegd uitschreef, leiden tot een spannende strijd tussen het linkse en rechtse blok. Analisten speculeren dat de vervroeging te maken had met ...

external.description: De Deense parlementsverkiezingen, die premier Mette Frederiksen vervroegd uitschreef, leiden tot een spannende strijd tussen het linkse en rechtse blok. record.text: Don't take this as a defense of AI translation! For the question of whether AI *should* produce translations, my answer is a firm no. But the question here is whether AI *can* produce translations of Latin, which I must admit it can.

I have problems with machine translation on an ethical/philosophical level. But practically, the translations are generally fine, even for Latin. I took the Cicero quote and swapped some of the word pairs (in case it was trained on this quote), and ChatGPT, Claude, and Google Translate all did OK.

For there is much said about the places and abodes of the gods, as well as about their forms and their manner of life, and there is great disagreement among philosophers about these matters. But the question that most of all contains the essence and cause of the issue is this: whether they do nothing, undertake nothing, and are free from all administration and care of things, or whether, on the contrary, all things were established and created by them from the beginning and are moved and governed by them for an infinite time. On this point there is, above all, great disagreement; and unless it is decided, it is necessary that human beings remain in ignorance about the greatest matters and in the utmost error.

"For indeed many things are said both about the places and abodes of the gods, and about their appearances and their manner of life; and concerning these matters there is great disagreement among philosophers and much dispute. But what most of all contains the heart of the matter — whether the gods do nothing, attempt nothing, and are free from all administration and governance of affairs, or whether on the contrary all things were established and made by them from the beginning and are moved and governed by them unto infinite time — this above all is a great disagreement, and unless it is decided, men must necessarily remain in ignorance of the most important things and in the deepest error."

For much is said about the places and seats of the gods, and about the figures and the action of life, and philosophers dispute about this with the greatest disagreement; but what most concerns the thing and cause, whether they do nothing, attempt nothing, are free from all administration and care of things, or whether, on the contrary, all things were established and made by them from the beginning and are moved and governed to an infinite time, is a great disagreement in the first place, and unless it is settled, men must be ignorant of the greatest things and be in the greatest error.

But what most of all contains the heart of the matter — whether the gods do nothing, attempt nothing, and are free from all administration and governance of affairs, or whether on the contrary all things were established and made by them from the beginning and are moved and governed by them unto infinite time — this above all is a great disagreement, and unless it is decided, men must necessarily remain in ignorance of the most important things and in the deepest error."

For much is said about the places and seats of the gods, and about the figures and the action of life, and philosophers dispute about this with the greatest disagreement; but what most concerns the thing and cause, whether they do nothing, attempt nothing, are free from all administration and care of things, or whether, on the contrary, all things were established and made by them from the beginning and are moved and governed to an infinite time, is a great disagreement in the first place, and unless it is settled, men must be ignorant of the greatest things and be in the greatest error.

#FilmSky #MovieSky

I'd rather live on Interstellar's Earth than in Looper's Kansas City, or IRL Kansas City, frankly.

images.alt: We're also seeing a lot of the action from the first third of the film from a second perspective now, which, like, I totally get it. I've seen movies. But it's not interesting or original. Most of it wasn't entertaining to watch the first time, much less a second.

He's making only dumb faces in Looper, and what's metatextually hilarious is that it clearly informed Joseph Gordon-Levitt's performance and the digital trickery (it's not very tricksy), but it's also objectively not any good, either. It reminds me of Kraven the Hunter, where clearly there were efforts made to match Russell Crowe's energy and performance, and it failed there, too.
It's a LOT of this.

record.text: NEWS: Grammarly tells me it will let "experts" like me opt out of having their names used against their will and for no compensation as part of its "expert review" feature https://www.platformer.news/grammarly-expert-review-reviewed/

images.alt:
Grammarly declined my request to interview CEO Shishir Mehrotra today. But it told me that in response to criticisms, it will allow experts to opt out of the feature by emailing expertoptout@superhuman.com.

The company gave me this statement over email:

We’ve heard the feedback about this tool and appreciate the engagement from those who have taken the time to raise thoughtful questions about the functionality and the experts surfaced. We agree that the product experience can be improved for both users and experts. The agent was designed to help users discover influential perspectives and scholarship that add value to their work. We want the people behind those perspectives to have greater control over whether their name is used, while providing new ways for influential voices to reach new audiences. Our goal is to improve Expert Review to deliver this outcome.

Rollout begi...

external.description:
**[Introduction]**

We’re introducing a **new capability in _Workforce Insights agent_ and _Microsoft 365 Copilot_ **that allows organizations**to customize how managers are identified**. This enhancement uses the **_SupervisorIndicator_** attribute from the Microsoft Organizational Data Ingestion System (MODIS) to align Copilot responses, insights, and analytics with your organization’s formal manager definition. This helps ensure accurate and consistent manager‑related metrics without altering your reporting hierarchy or access controls.

**[When this will happen]**

  * **Frontier Preview:** Rollout begins in **late March 2026.**



**[How this affects your organization]**

_Who is affected_

  * Organizations using Workforce Insights agent or Microsoft 365 Copilot with organizational data provided through MODIS



 _What will happen_

  * If your tenant already provides the** _SupervisorIndicator_** attribute through MODIS, Workforce Insights agent and Microsoft 365 Copilot will automatically begin using it to identify managers.
  * Many organizations define “manager” differently based on HR policies (for example, including contractors or interns, or excluding certain job families). This capability allows your organization’s definition to take precedence over the default hierarchy‑based definition.
  * When **_SupervisorIndicator_** is present, it becomes the **authoritative signal** for manager status used in:
    * manager‑related questions and responses,
    * organizational insights,
    * analytics and metrics (such as manager counts and manager‑to‑IC ratios).
  * This change aligns Copilot insights and analytics with your HR‑defined manager criteria **without impacting** :
    * reporting hierarchy,
    * manager–employee relationships,
    * access controls,
    * delegation,
    * privacy enforcement.
  * If your tenant does**not** provide the **_SupervisorIndicator_** attribute, manager identification will continue using organizational hierarchy (employees with direct reports).
  * The feature is **enabled automatically** when the attribute is present—no admin toggle is required.



**[What you can do to prepare]**

If your tenant uses **_SupervisorIndicator_** , ask your organizational data admin to:




  * Review the values in this attribute to ensure accuracy for Copilot and Workforce Insights queries.
  * Ensure your organization has **at least 50 active Microsoft 365 Copilot licenses**.



No other admin action is required at this time.

**Learn more:**

  * Use your organizational data in Microsoft 365 and Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn
  * Workforce Insights agent | Microsoft 365 Copilot | Microsoft 365 | Microsoft Learn



**[Compliance considerations]**

No compliance considerations identified. Review as appropriate for your organization.
external.title: MC1260710: Microsoft 365 Copilot: Customize how managers are identified in Workforce Insights agent and Copilot responses

external.description:
**[Introduction]**

You’re receiving this message because your tenant is opted in to the _AI in SharePoint public preview_ (formerly SharePoint Knowledge Agent). As part of **this public preview** , we’re rolling out a **new AI-powered site creation experience** and a**fallback model path to OpenAI**.

**[When this will happen]**

  * Create SharePoint sites with AI: **April 2026**
  * **Open AI fallback model option: March 2026**(rolling out **now**)



**[Who is affected]**

  * Microsoft 365 Copilot–licensed users in tenants opted into the AI in _SharePoint public preview_**.**
  * **Create sites with AI:** Available to users in preview tenants who already have permission to create sites, and only in tenants configured for the All Sites or Exclude Selected Sites preview options. **Note:** Tenants using the Selected Sites configuration do not have access to this feature during preview.
  * **Fallback model option:** Applies to preview tenants, including those that do not allow Anthropic as an AI subprocessor. In these tenants, AI in SharePoint may use a fallback reasoning model when Anthropic is not enabled.



**[How this will affect your organization]**

  * **Create sites with AI:** This update introduces an AI‑assisted planning experience that lets eligible users describe a business need, refine a site plan through chat, and then create a SharePoint site. The experience honors existing site‑creation permissions and is available only in tenants opted into the AI in SharePoint public preview using the All Sites or Exclude Selected Sites configuration. It can be accessed from **_SharePoint Start (Build)_** and the _**+ New site experience**_ in the _SharePoint admin center_.
  * **Fallback model option:** AI in _SharePoint’s refreshed experience_ uses an Anthropic reasoning model for advanced, multi‑step planning and execution. When Anthropic is not enabled, AI in SharePoint may use a fallback reasoning model to maintain preview availability, and some advanced capabilities may vary.



_Admin experience:_

__

_Site planning experience:_

_Start build page:_

**[What you need to do to prepare]**

  * **Create sites with AI:** Ensure your tenant is opted into the _AI in SharePoint public preview_ using the **_All Sites_** or** _Exclude Selected Sites_** configuration. To access the full preview experience, including advanced multi‑step planning and execution, **Anthropic must be enabled as an AI subprocessor**. If not, AI in SharePoint may use a fallback reasoning model to maintain preview availability, but some advanced capabilities may vary. If Anthropic is already enabled, no action is required. Communicate to eligible users how to discover and use this capability.
  * **Fallback model option:** No action is required. If Anthropic is not enabled, AI in SharePoint may use a fallback reasoning model to help maintain preview availability, though some advanced capabilities may vary depending on the model used.



**Additional information**

  * This preview experience is part of the **Microsoft 365 Copilot license**.
  * AI in SharePoint capabilities require sites to be explicitly opted into the _AI in SharePoint public preview_ , or for the tenant to be opted in at the tenant level. Sites that are not opted in will not receive preview benefits.



Learn more:

  * AI in SharePoint: From intent to real solutions - Introducing new agentic building in SharePoint and more updates | Microsoft SharePoint Blog
  * Anthropic as a subprocessor for Microsoft Online Services | Microsoft 365 Copilot | Microsoft 365 | Microsoft Learn
  * Building sites with AI | SharePoint in Microsoft 365 | SharePoint | Microsoft Learn
  * Get started with AI in SharePoint (preview) | SharePoint in Microsoft 365 | SharePoint | Microsoft Learn

record.text: Microsoft Fabric's OneLake introduces storage tiers (hot, cool, cold) and updates operation reporting by including tier names, consolidating proxy/redirect operations, and grouping by workspace in the Capacity Metrics app. Billing rates remain unchanged. Changes start April 1, 2026; item-level de...

external.description:
**[Introduction]**

We're introducing **OneLake storage tiers**(hot, cool, and cold) in _Microsoft Fabric_. As part of this rollout, we are updating how OneLake compute operations appear in your capacity billing. These updates improve clarity and align reporting with the new storage tier model. **There is no change to billing rates.**

_Key updates include_

  * **Operation names include the storage tier.** For example, “ _OneLake Read via Proxy_ ” becomes “ _OneLake Read (Hot)_.”
  * **Proxy and Redirect operations are consolidated.** Operations previously reported separately will appear under a single operation name. Because consumption rates are the same, this has **no billing impact**.
  * **Operations are reported at the workspace level.** A new OneLake item in the __ Fabric Capacity Metrics app groups operations by workspace instead of individual items. For item-level detail, use OneLake diagnostics to understand usage across Fabric and non-Fabric workloads.



Units of measure and consumption rates remain unchanged.

**[When this will happen]**

  * Operation name updates **begin April 1, 2026**.
  * Operation consolidation and Metrics app updates will roll out over the following weeks.



**[How this affects your organization]**

_Who is affected_

  * All organizations using Microsoft Fabric with OneLake storage and capacity billing



 _What will happen_

  * **Operation names** will include the applicable storage tier (for example, “Hot”):
  * **Proxy and Redirect operations** will appear under a single consolidated operation name:
  * **OneLake operations** will be grouped by workspace under a new OneLake item in the Fabric Capacity Metrics app.
  * **Item-level compute reporting** will move to OneLake diagnostics.



The following example illustrates how the report now appears:

There is **no change** to billing rates or your total costs.

_Operation name changes:_

**[What you can do to prepare]**

  * **Review any custom analytics or automation.** Update operation names in reports, dashboards, and scripts to align with the new naming convention **before April 1, 2026**.
  * **Plan for workspace-level reporting.** If you rely on item-level operation details in the _Capacity Metrics app_ , configure OneLake diagnostics to maintain that granularity.
  * Review the documentation for OneLake compute consumption in _Microsoft Fabric_.
  * Share with your helpdesk and reporting teams if they monitor capacity or cost analytics.



No other action is required.

**Learn more:**

  * Install the Microsoft Fabric capacity metrics app | Microsoft Fabric | Microsoft Learn

  * OneLake compute and storage consumption | OneLake | Microsoft Fabric | Microsoft Learn
  * OneLake diagnostics | OneLake | Microsoft Fabric | Microsoft Learn
  * **OneLake storage tiers** - Microsoft Fabric Roadmap
  * OneLake, the OneDrive for data | OneLake | Microsoft Fabric | Microsoft Learn



**[Compliance considerations]**

No compliance considerations identified. Review as appropriate for your organization.

**[Support]**

For assistance, contact your Microsoft account team or visit Microsoft Fabric support.

value.text:
"The AI Grad Student": A Harvard professor describes working with Claude. 

Early on, he describes misconduct that would cause any student to be terminated: "It faked results, hoping I wouldn't notice."

But he ends the essay with "Now I'm doing 100% of my research with LLMs".

Am I losing my mind?

images.alt: The more I dug, the more I found it had been tweaking things left and right. Claude had been adjusting parameters to make plots match rather than finding actual errors. It faked results, hoping I wouldn’t notice.

In addition to the growth in interest, the tools themselves have been steadily improving. I am now doing 100% of my research with LLMs. I don't encapsulate the LaTeX writing anymore since I actually enjoy writing papers and it helps me think, and I still write some Mathematica code on my own. But I haven’t compiled anything myself on the command line in months. I typically have four to five projects running at once and go between windows checking the output and sending a new prompt. It feels a bit like Magnus Carlsen taking on five grandmasters in parallel. People have asked me why I’m not writing a paper every two weeks. The answer is I don’t see why I should. I am growing intellectually—learning so much every day—and trying out some ambitious problems, most of which fail. I anticipate the floodgates will open very soon.

The more I dug, the more I found it had been tweaking things left and right. Claude had been adjusting parameters to make plots match rather than finding actual errors. It faked results, hoping I wouldn’t notice.

In addition to the growth in interest, the tools themselves have been steadily improving. I am now doing 100% of my research with LLMs. I don't encapsulate the LaTeX writing anymore since I actually enjoy writing papers and it helps me think, and I still write some Mathematica code on my own. But I haven’t compiled anything myself on the command line in months. I typically have four to five projects running at once and go between windows checking the output and sending a new prompt. It feels a bit like Magnus Carlsen taking on five grandmasters in parallel. People have asked me why I’m not writing a paper every two weeks. The answer is I don’t see why I should. I am growing intellectually—learning so much every day—and trying out some ambitious problems, most of which fail. I anticipate the floodgates will open very soon.

From the Editor:

Welcome to the latest Python on Microcontrollers newsletter! The last issue was rather traditional in respect to the news. This week there is a bounty of content where Python intersects AI and LLMs. Not in an evil “AI will write all the code” but in a benevolent “LLMs can help you do things better” approach.

Scanning Your GitHub Uploads for Data Loss Via the GitHub MCP Server

Secret scanning in AI coding agents via the GitHub MCP Server

It can happen to any of us. We’re posting our MicroPython/CircuitPython/Python code to GitHub. And there is one file, settings.toml, secrets.py, etc. that we put our password in to a weather API, Adafruit IO, WiFi, etc. It should have been posted generically, but wasn’t, and now your credentials are on GitHub forever. What to do? A new feature of GitHub Copilot allows a search for such files in a commit and warns you. It could save heartache.

(attached is a picture of the AI bot detecting secrets)

value.text: Crimson Desert - statement on usage of AI

images.alt:
We would like to address questions regarding the use of AI in Crimson Desert.

During development, some 2D visual props were created as part of early-stage iteration using experimental AI generative tools. These assets helped us rapidly explore tone and atmosphere in the earlier phases of production. However, our intention has always been for any such assets to be replaced, following final work and review by our art and development teams, with work that aligned with our quality standards and creative direction.

Following reports from our community, we have identified that some of these assets were unintentionally included in the final release. This is not in line with our internal standards, and we take full responsibility for it.

We also acknowledge that we should have clearly disclosed our use of AI. While these tools were primarily used during early production, with the expectation that these assets would be replaced prior to release, we recognize that this does not excuse the lack of transparency.

We sincerely apologize for these oversights.

We are currently conducting a comprehensive audit of all in-game assets and are taking steps to replace any affected content. Updated assets will be rolled out in upcoming patches. In parallel, we are reviewing and strengthening our internal processes to ensure greater transparency and consistency in how we communicate with players moving forward.
